aiTitle: Review federation login workflow and cloud secret handling

content.text: Found it — `memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` worked exactly this through (§7–§8, resolved in R4/R5). Let me read those sections so I report the actual conclusion, not a paraphrase.

content.content:
171	## 7. The right axis: human-driven vs unattended (not org vs no-org)
172	
173	A tempting but wrong split is "no-org mode" vs "with-org mode." It is wrong
174	because **org-ness is not the project's to control** — it is a property of each
175	user's account, so branching on it means defending behavior the project cannot
176	predict.
177	
178	The defensible axis is the *workload*, which the project does control:
179	
180	- **Human-driven** (a person at a workstation, even a headless one): can always
181	  do the browser/device authentication and periodic reauth. Impersonation fits,
182	  on any org, **without needing to administer that org** — granting impersonation
183	  rights is an IAM binding the depot's own Governor controls, not an org-policy
184	  override requiring org-admin.
185	- **Unattended** (Cloud Build, CI with nobody present): cannot reauth, so it
186	  needs a *machine* identity (workload identity / build SA) — keyless, and
187	  org-sanctioned — regardless of org.
188	
189	Same two modes whether the user is on gmail or a Workspace never seen before.
190	Fewer permutations, keyed on something stable and owned.
191	
192	---
193	
194	## 8. The synthetic-human CI complication (the genuinely hard corner)
195	
196	The clean axis above has one exception specific to this project: **its
197	cross-platform CI must behave *as if a human* operates it** (Windows / macOS /
198	Linux), because the point is to validate the human-operator experience. A machine
199	identity would exercise a code path no real human walks. So this CI is
200	**unattended *and* must wear a human face** — a "synthetic human" the clean axis
201	does not allow for.
202	
203	Resolution, with honest costs:
204	
205	- It runs on an org **the project administers** (its own test org), so the
206	  session policy can be set long / never-expiring there, and the synthetic-human
207	  credential can be emplaced before a run and survive it untouched. **Contained
208	  to the project's own yard; never part of the shipped model.**
209	- "Very long lived" is a privilege grantable only on orgs you administer.
210	  End-users on strict orgs get *their* org's cadence, full stop.
211	- **Residual cost:** a long-lived human refresh token sitting on a CI box before
212	  a run is still a durable secret — a milder, revocable one, but durable. This is
213	  the one place the project does not fully escape "a long-lived secret on a
214	  machine." Acceptable for the test rig; not held up as the org-native ideal.
215	
216	Practical near-term mitigation (independent of any rework): the reauth window
217	only governs the *human Payor* OAuth; the long, slow parts of a run (cloud
218	builds via the Director SA, crucibles) use long-lived SA credentials and have no
219	reauth window. So a fresh authentication (or a cheap preflight probe that
220	attempts a token refresh) **right before** a run covers the human-touch prefix,
221	and the long tail rides credentials immune to the cadence.
222	
223	---
224	
225	## 9. Authentication mechanics: OOB is deprecated; Device flow is the successor
226	
227	- The "paste this URL in a browser, authenticate, paste the code back" step is the
228	  **OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant via the out-of-band (OOB) redirect**
229	  (`urn:ietf:wg:oauth:2.0:oob`). The pasted value is the *authorization code*.
230	  This is what `rbw-gPI` does today.
231	- **Google has deprecated OOB.** Building further on it is building on sand.
232	- The modern, non-deprecated, headless-friendly successor is the **OAuth 2.0
233	  Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628)** — the "go to this URL, enter this code"
234	  smart-TV flow. The browser need not be on the box; it need only exist somewhere
235	  the operator can reach.
236	- Crucially, the device flow **authenticates a human and produces a token tied to
237	  that human's identity** — the same identity model as OOB, just a better
238	  delivery channel. It introduces no new credential type and no authority-bearing
239	  artifact. Authority still lives in central IAM. It does **not** recreate the
240	  RBRA mess.
241	
242	---
243	
244	## 10. Acceptance in orgs the project does not control
245	
246	The acid test. In the target model:
247	
248	- A real human user on *any* org authenticates via the device flow, reauthing
249	  when their org's policy demands it (a human is present — fine).
250	- The admin of *their* org grants/revokes the person's roles centrally; the
251	  project ships no keys.
252	- The project never needs to weaken the user's org policy, because impersonation
253	  creates no SA keys — it sidesteps `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` entirely.
254	
255	This is why the reorientation matters for the project's success: it is the
256	difference between "works only where I am the org admin" and "works in any
257	serious org out of the box."
258	
259	---
260	
261	## 11. Open questions (to decide deliberately, feeding ₢BSAAF)
262	
263	- **Session-policy as a lever vs a constraint.** For the project's own test org,
264	  what should the Cloud session control be set to, and how is that documented as
265	  test-rig-only rather than model behavior? (Verify the current
266	  `scaleinvariant.org` setting — it lives in the Workspace Admin console.)
267	- **One human credential vs anything per-role.** Confirm the single-identity +
268	  impersonation-pointer shape, and what (if anything) the per-role RBRA file
269	  still holds once it carries no secret.
270	- **Does Payor stay distinct**, or does it become "a human granted the payor
271	  role" under the same model?
272	- **Coexistence & migration.** How RBRA-keyfile mode and impersonation mode
273	  coexist during transition (the 2026-04-27 memo's mode field), and whether
274	  keyfile mode is retained only for the consumer/bootstrap case.
275	- **The synthetic-human CI emplacement** mechanics, and where the residual
276	  long-lived-secret risk is documented and bounded.
277	- **Probe / handbook / regime surfaces** that assume keyfiles
278	  (RBSAJ/RBSAO/RBSAV, onboarding tracks, `rbw-gPR`'s stale "6-month idle" text).
279	- **The gauntlet coupling.** Any pace that levies a fresh depot (the full
280	  gauntlet) re-hits the key wall on the new project; skirmish (standing depot)
281	  does not. Regression during burn-down should lean on skirmish.
282	
283	---
284	
285	## 12. The one-line version
286	
287	The org didn't break a setting — it invalidated the assumption the credential
288	model was built on. RBRA keyfiles are the consumer/bootstrap mode. The org-native
289	mode is: a human proves identity (device flow), the project holds no role
290	secrets, roles are reached by impersonation, and authority is granted and revoked
291	centrally in IAM. Reauth is not the obstacle; it is the compass that forces the
292	model to be honest.
293	
294	---
295	
296	## 13. Resolutions (decided)
297	
298	Decided answers to §11, banked as they settle. Implementation heat TBD.
299	
300	### R1 — Payor stays distinct; the three lower tiers unify (resolves §11 "Does Payor stay distinct?")

file.content:
## 7. The right axis: human-driven vs unattended (not org vs no-org)

A tempting but wrong split is "no-org mode" vs "with-org mode." It is wrong
because **org-ness is not the project's to control** — it is a property of each
user's account, so branching on it means defending behavior the project cannot
predict.

The defensible axis is the *workload*, which the project does control:

- **Human-driven** (a person at a workstation, even a headless one): can always
  do the browser/device authentication and periodic reauth. Impersonation fits,
  on any org, **without needing to administer that org** — granting impersonation
  rights is an IAM binding the depot's own Governor controls, not an org-policy
  override requiring org-admin.
- **Unattended** (Cloud Build, CI with nobody present): cannot reauth, so it
  needs a *machine* identity (workload identity / build SA) — keyless, and
  org-sanctioned — regardless of org.

Same two modes whether the user is on gmail or a Workspace never seen before.
Fewer permutations, keyed on something stable and owned.

---

## 8. The synthetic-human CI complication (the genuinely hard corner)

The clean axis above has one exception specific to this project: **its
cross-platform CI must behave *as if a human* operates it** (Windows / macOS /
Linux), because the point is to validate the human-operator experience. A machine
identity would exercise a code path no real human walks. So this CI is
**unattended *and* must wear a human face** — a "synthetic human" the clean axis
does not allow for.

Resolution, with honest costs:

- It runs on an org **the project administers** (its own test org), so the
  session policy can be set long / never-expiring there, and the synthetic-human
  credential can be emplaced before a run and survive it untouched. **Contained
  to the project's own yard; never part of the shipped model.**
- "Very long lived" is a privilege grantable only on orgs you administer.
  End-users on strict orgs get *their* org's cadence, full stop.
- **Residual cost:** a long-lived human refresh token sitting on a CI box before
  a run is still a durable secret — a milder, revocable one, but durable. This is
  the one place the project does not fully escape "a long-lived secret on a
  machine." Acceptable for the test rig; not held up as the org-native ideal.

Practical near-term mitigation (independent of any rework): the reauth window
only governs the *human Payor* OAuth; the long, slow parts of a run (cloud
builds via the Director SA, crucibles) use long-lived SA credentials and have no
reauth window. So a fresh authentication (or a cheap preflight probe that
attempts a token refresh) **right before** a run covers the human-touch prefix,
and the long tail rides credentials immune to the cadence.

---

## 9. Authentication mechanics: OOB is deprecated; Device flow is the successor

- The "paste this URL in a browser, authenticate, paste the code back" step is the
  **OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant via the out-of-band (OOB) redirect**
  (`urn:ietf:wg:oauth:2.0:oob`). The pasted value is the *authorization code*.
  This is what `rbw-gPI` does today.
- **Google has deprecated OOB.** Building further on it is building on sand.
- The modern, non-deprecated, headless-friendly successor is the **OAuth 2.0
  Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628)** — the "go to this URL, enter this code"
  smart-TV flow. The browser need not be on the box; it need only exist somewhere
  the operator can reach.
- Crucially, the device flow **authenticates a human and produces a token tied to
  that human's identity** — the same identity model as OOB, just a better
  delivery channel. It introduces no new credential type and no authority-bearing
  artifact. Authority still lives in central IAM. It does **not** recreate the
  RBRA mess.

---

## 10. Acceptance in orgs the project does not control

The acid test. In the target model:

- A real human user on *any* org authenticates via the device flow, reauthing
  when their org's policy demands it (a human is present — fine).
- The admin of *their* org grants/revokes the person's roles centrally; the
  project ships no keys.
- The project never needs to weaken the user's org policy, because impersonation
  creates no SA keys — it sidesteps `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` entirely.

This is why the reorientation matters for the project's success: it is the
difference between "works only where I am the org admin" and "works in any
serious org out of the box."

---

## 11. Open questions (to decide deliberately, feeding ₢BSAAF)

- **Session-policy as a lever vs a constraint.** For the project's own test org,
  what should the Cloud session control be set to, and how is that documented as
  test-rig-only rather than model behavior? (Verify the current
  `scaleinvariant.org` setting — it lives in the Workspace Admin console.)
- **One human credential vs anything per-role.** Confirm the single-identity +
  impersonation-pointer shape, and what (if anything) the per-role RBRA file
  still holds once it carries no secret.
- **Does Payor stay distinct**, or does it become "a human granted the payor
  role" under the same model?
- **Coexistence & migration.** How RBRA-keyfile mode and impersonation mode
  coexist during transition (the 2026-04-27 memo's mode field), and whether
  keyfile mode is retained only for the consumer/bootstrap case.
- **The synthetic-human CI emplacement** mechanics, and where the residual
  long-lived-secret risk is documented and bounded.
- **Probe / handbook / regime surfaces** that assume keyfiles
  (RBSAJ/RBSAO/RBSAV, onboarding tracks, `rbw-gPR`'s stale "6-month idle" text).
- **The gauntlet coupling.** Any pace that levies a fresh depot (the full
  gauntlet) re-hits the key wall on the new project; skirmish (standing depot)
  does not. Regression during burn-down should lean on skirmish.

---

## 12. The one-line version

The org didn't break a setting — it invalidated the assumption the credential
model was built on. RBRA keyfiles are the consumer/bootstrap mode. The org-native
mode is: a human proves identity (device flow), the project holds no role
secrets, roles are reached by impersonation, and authority is granted and revoked
centrally in IAM. Reauth is not the obstacle; it is the compass that forces the
model to be honest.

---

## 13. Resolutions (decided)

Decided answers to §11, banked as they settle. Implementation heat TBD.

### R1 — Payor stays distinct; the three lower tiers unify (resolves §11 "Does Payor stay distinct?")

content.content:
400	Decision: MVP ships RBRA (no rework). The keyless successor is **deferred and
401	unchosen** — do not commit build order while the complexity budget is tight. Bank
402	the map, choose the tier when org adoption actually demands keyless.
403	
404	Note: this federation is *ingress* identity (operators reaching the depot), distinct
405	from the roadmap's envoy entry, which uses federation for *egress* (GCP→AWS
406	cross-post). Same mechanism family, opposite direction — keep them unconflated.
407	
408	### R4 — Synthetic-human CI emplacement and test-org session policy (resolves §11 "synthetic-human CI emplacement" and "session-policy as a lever vs constraint")
409	
410	The two test-rig questions resolve together. A federated operator (Workforce
411	Identity Federation) authenticates by device flow; the resulting Google session
412	is capped at **12 hours** — a hard GCP limit, even on an org you own. So
413	month-scale unattended test machines are *not* a long session: they are a
414	**persisted IdP refresh token** that the machine silently re-exchanges every
415	≤12 h, headless. The human touch happens once, when the token is minted.
416	
417	Decision:
418	
419	- **Shipped human roles persist no refresh token** — device flow per session,
420	  reauth on the operator-org's own cadence. This is the org-native ideal.
421	- **The project's own test rig** (firewalled machines, project-administered test
422	  org) gets a **test-only enrollment** that persists one refresh token per
423	  machine — the single acknowledged durable-secret residual (§8). Set the test
424	  org's Workforce session to its 12 h maximum; the month-span comes from the
425	  refresh token, not the session.
426	- **Not security-breaking:** a federated/OAuth refresh token is not an SA key
427	  (org-legal, no policy override needed), is centrally revocable (disable the
428	  principal at the IdP — kills every machine at once), is capability-scoped, and
429	  is physically contained behind the firewall.
430	- This is a **federation-era** mechanism. Under MVP/keyfile the same durable
431	  test-credential need is met by the SA key itself (where key creation is
432	  permitted). Two eras, one need.
433	
434	Session policy is therefore a lever pulled only on the org you administer (the
435	test rig), documented as test-rig-only because end users always get *their* org's
436	cadence — never a project-set value.
437	
438	### R5 — Gauntlet coupling: regression leans on skirmish (resolves §11 "gauntlet coupling")
439	
440	Independent of credential tier. A full-gauntlet run levies a fresh depot, which
441	re-hits `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` on a secure-by-default org **even in
442	RBRA/keyfile mode**. Skirmish (standing depot) does not re-levy and does not hit
443	the wall.
444	
445	Decision: burn-down and routine regression **lean on skirmish**; full-gauntlet
446	fresh-depot levies run only on an account that permits SA-key creation (no-org,
447	or an org with the override). This is an operational testing posture, live for
448	MVP — not contingent on any keyless work.
449	
450	### §11 disposition (closure)
451	
452	All seven §11 questions are now decided or deliberately retired:
453	
454	| §11 question | Disposition |
455	|---|---|
456	| One human credential vs per-role | Resolved — R2 |
457	| Does Payor stay distinct | Resolved — R1 / R2 |
458	| Coexistence & migration | Resolved — R3 (mode-enum migration; see the credential-models memo) |
459	| Synthetic-human CI emplacement | Resolved — R4 |
460	| Session-policy lever vs constraint | Resolved — R4 |
461	| Gauntlet coupling | Resolved — R5 |
462	| Probe / handbook / regime keyfile surfaces (RBSAJ/RBSAO/RBSAV, onboarding, `rbw-gPR`) | **Retired** — R3 ships RBRA for MVP, so these surfaces stay correct as-is; no MVP rework. Re-decided if/when the keyless tier is chosen; the revisit-trigger lives in the RBSHR "Operator federation" roadmap entry. |
463	
464	The federation tier's mechanism — and the credential mode-enum and cult-verb
465	shape — are recorded separately in

file.content:
Decision: MVP ships RBRA (no rework). The keyless successor is **deferred and
unchosen** — do not commit build order while the complexity budget is tight. Bank
the map, choose the tier when org adoption actually demands keyless.

Note: this federation is *ingress* identity (operators reaching the depot), distinct
from the roadmap's envoy entry, which uses federation for *egress* (GCP→AWS
cross-post). Same mechanism family, opposite direction — keep them unconflated.

### R4 — Synthetic-human CI emplacement and test-org session policy (resolves §11 "synthetic-human CI emplacement" and "session-policy as a lever vs constraint")

The two test-rig questions resolve together. A federated operator (Workforce
Identity Federation) authenticates by device flow; the resulting Google session
is capped at **12 hours** — a hard GCP limit, even on an org you own. So
month-scale unattended test machines are *not* a long session: they are a
**persisted IdP refresh token** that the machine silently re-exchanges every
≤12 h, headless. The human touch happens once, when the token is minted.

Decision:

- **Shipped human roles persist no refresh token** — device flow per session,
  reauth on the operator-org's own cadence. This is the org-native ideal.
- **The project's own test rig** (firewalled machines, project-administered test
  org) gets a **test-only enrollment** that persists one refresh token per
  machine — the single acknowledged durable-secret residual (§8). Set the test
  org's Workforce session to its 12 h maximum; the month-span comes from the
  refresh token, not the session.
- **Not security-breaking:** a federated/OAuth refresh token is not an SA key
  (org-legal, no policy override needed), is centrally revocable (disable the
  principal at the IdP — kills every machine at once), is capability-scoped, and
  is physically contained behind the firewall.
- This is a **federation-era** mechanism. Under MVP/keyfile the same durable
  test-credential need is met by the SA key itself (where key creation is
  permitted). Two eras, one need.

Session policy is therefore a lever pulled only on the org you administer (the
test rig), documented as test-rig-only because end users always get *their* org's
cadence — never a project-set value.

### R5 — Gauntlet coupling: regression leans on skirmish (resolves §11 "gauntlet coupling")

Independent of credential tier. A full-gauntlet run levies a fresh depot, which
re-hits `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` on a secure-by-default org **even in
RBRA/keyfile mode**. Skirmish (standing depot) does not re-levy and does not hit
the wall.

Decision: burn-down and routine regression **lean on skirmish**; full-gauntlet
fresh-depot levies run only on an account that permits SA-key creation (no-org,
or an org with the override). This is an operational testing posture, live for
MVP — not contingent on any keyless work.

### §11 disposition (closure)

All seven §11 questions are now decided or deliberately retired:

| §11 question | Disposition |
|---|---|
| One human credential vs per-role | Resolved — R2 |
| Does Payor stay distinct | Resolved — R1 / R2 |
| Coexistence & migration | Resolved — R3 (mode-enum migration; see the credential-models memo) |
| Synthetic-human CI emplacement | Resolved — R4 |
| Session-policy lever vs constraint | Resolved — R4 |
| Gauntlet coupling | Resolved — R5 |
| Probe / handbook / regime keyfile surfaces (RBSAJ/RBSAO/RBSAV, onboarding, `rbw-gPR`) | **Retired** — R3 ships RBRA for MVP, so these surfaces stay correct as-is; no MVP rework. Re-decided if/when the keyless tier is chosen; the revisit-trigger lives in the RBSHR "Operator federation" roadmap entry. |

content.text:
Found it — and it's not just a stray thought, it's a *decided* resolution. The reasoning lives in `memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` (§7–§8, banked as **R4** and **R5**). Here's the conclusion, faithfully.

## The reframe that dissolves most of the problem

The memo's first move (§7) is to reject "org vs no-org" as the axis and use **human-driven vs unattended** instead — because that's what the project actually controls. And once you split that way, most of an "automated test run" stops being a human-presence problem at all:

- The **long tail of a run** — cloud builds via the Director SA, crucibles — already runs on **machine identities** (workload identity / build SA), keyless, **with no reauth window**. Federation never touches these. They're immune to the human-present question by construction.
- Only the **human-operator prefix** of a run is the issue.

So "a human can't be present in CI" is true but narrow: it bites exactly one slice, not the whole run.

## The genuinely hard corner (§8) — and why it's hard for *this* project specifically

The project's own cross-platform CI is special: its **whole point is to validate the human-operator experience** (Windows/macOS/Linux). If CI authenticated as a machine identity, it would exercise a code path **no real human walks** — validating the wrong thing. So this CI is *unattended* **and** must *wear a human face*: a **"synthetic human,"** which the clean axis doesn't allow for.

## The resolution (R4) — and it's exactly the tension from your last question

This is the canonical "persist the refresh token" case, and the memo draws the line precisely where I drew it for your weekly-director scenario:

- **Shipped human operators persist no refresh token** — device flow per session, reauth on their org's own cadence. The org-native ideal.
- **The project's own test rig** (firewalled machines, project-administered test org) gets a **test-only enrollment** that persists **one IdP refresh token per machine** — *"the single acknowledged durable-secret residual."* The machine silently re-exchanges it every ≤12h (the hard GCP session cap), so the **human touch happens once, when the token is minted**; the month-span comes from the refresh token, not from any long session.

And it's bounded, not waved away — R4 lists why it's *"not security-breaking"*: a refresh token is **not an SA key** (org-legal, no `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` override needed), is **centrally revocable** (disable the principal at the IdP → kills every machine at once), is **capability-scoped**, and is **physically firewall-contained**.

> **This is the direct answer to your previous question.** Your "director logs in weekly, runs many tabtargets" is *structurally identical* to the synthetic-human CI: both want headless multi-operation from one human touch, both require the persisted refresh token, both land on the **test-rig side** of the line. The memo's stance is that this posture is legitimate *only* on an org you administer behind a firewall — not the shipped human path. A real operator who wants weekly login on *their* org would be reproducing the test-rig's durable-secret residual on an uncontrolled machine, which is exactly what the tier refuses.

## Two practical riders the memo bolts on

- **Mitigation (§8 tail):** the reauth window governs *only* the human Payor OAuth. So a fresh auth — or a cheap preflight token-refresh probe — **right before** a run covers the human-touch prefix, and the long tail rides SA credentials immune to the cadence. Cheap and live today, independent of any keyless rework.
- **Gauntlet coupling (R5), orthogonal but bites the same place:** a full-gauntlet run levies a *fresh depot*, which re-hits `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` on a secure org **even in keyfile mode**. Decision: routine regression **leans on skirmish** (standing depot, no re-levy); fresh-depot gauntlet runs only on an account that permits SA-key creation. This is operational posture, live for MVP, not contingent on federation.

## The one-line version

A human *is* present in test runs — exactly once, to mint a refresh token onto a firewalled, project-owned test box; the box re-exchanges it headlessly every ≤12h, and the heavy machine work was never gated on a human anyway. Confined to the test rig, never the shipped human path.

---

This is federation-era (still out of ₣BZ's scope), but it sharpens the same BZ design input from last turn: the identity-keyed accessor's **credential source** must accommodate a refresh-token-backed source for the synthetic human, even though BZ only wires the keyfile source. Want me to (1) pull the synthetic-human refresh-token mechanics further — e.g. which IdP, the `offline_access` enrollment verb shape; or (2) capture this "CI = synthetic human, refresh-token-on-test-rig-only" conclusion as a ₣BZ paddock note so it's not re-derived a third time?

- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

289	## Citizen naming migration
290	
291	The model's foundation is the SA name becoming **identity-only** (`alice`, not `director-alice`) —
292	the precondition for one-citizen-one-key-union and for the lossy-IAM-derivation argument. (The
293	governor's analogous rename is in Governor teardown leak; this is the director/retriever dual.)
294	Surfaces:
295	
296	- **RBSDK / RBSRK** — SA-name composition drops the role prefix (was `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`).
297	- **RBSDD** — email synthesis becomes identity-only.
298	- **RBSDR** — the prefix-filter roster is retired in favor of the declared-ledger read.
299	- **RBSRA / RBSRR** — the three role-keyed `rbra.env` paths collapse toward one identity-keyed path.
300	
301	**Existing-SA cutover.** GCP cannot rename an SA, so legacy `director-*`/`retriever-*` SAs migrate by
302	delete-old + invest-new, re-entering the divest revoke-before-delete + the invest create/propagation
303	race (the memo-20260604 churn) — it must run through the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C tolerance.
304	
305	**One-time bootstrap** (distinct from the *retired* standing "derive from IAM"): recover each legacy
306	citizen's role — and thus its capability-set — from the role-prefixed SA **name** (the RBSDR roster
307	regex), reading bindings only to seed/confirm; thereafter the ledger stands independently. Stamp the
308	orphan marker onto each grandfathered SA so the sweep operates on a marker-uniform domain (a
309	back-stamp prerequisite — until then sweep coverage of the legacy population is incomplete).
310	
311	Whether *this heat* performs the migration is under the open scoping flag (the paddock).
312	
313	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
314	
315	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
316	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
317	  label.
318	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
319	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
320	- **A service account is capped at 10 keys** (hard, non-adjustable) — bounds the rekey lifecycle.
321	- A service account **can exist with zero IAM bindings** (the decoupling primitive).
322	
323	Sources:
324	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
325	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
326	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
327	
328	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
329	
330	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
331	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
332	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
333	
334	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
335	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
336	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
337	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
338	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
339	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
340	
341	A deferral-honoring breadcrumb has been added to memo-20260527's Status block now; the full
342	divergence note folds in when the model is frozen.

file.content:
## Citizen naming migration

The model's foundation is the SA name becoming **identity-only** (`alice`, not `director-alice`) —
the precondition for one-citizen-one-key-union and for the lossy-IAM-derivation argument. (The
governor's analogous rename is in Governor teardown leak; this is the director/retriever dual.)
Surfaces:

- **RBSDK / RBSRK** — SA-name composition drops the role prefix (was `${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>`).
- **RBSDD** — email synthesis becomes identity-only.
- **RBSDR** — the prefix-filter roster is retired in favor of the declared-ledger read.
- **RBSRA / RBSRR** — the three role-keyed `rbra.env` paths collapse toward one identity-keyed path.

**Existing-SA cutover.** GCP cannot rename an SA, so legacy `director-*`/`retriever-*` SAs migrate by
delete-old + invest-new, re-entering the divest revoke-before-delete + the invest create/propagation
race (the memo-20260604 churn) — it must run through the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C tolerance.

**One-time bootstrap** (distinct from the *retired* standing "derive from IAM"): recover each legacy
citizen's role — and thus its capability-set — from the role-prefixed SA **name** (the RBSDR roster
regex), reading bindings only to seed/confirm; thereafter the ledger stands independently. Stamp the
orphan marker onto each grandfathered SA so the sweep operates on a marker-uniform domain (a
back-stamp prerequisite — until then sweep coverage of the legacy population is incomplete).

Whether *this heat* performs the migration is under the open scoping flag (the paddock).

## Verified facts (checked 260605)

- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
  label.
- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
- **A service account is capped at 10 keys** (hard, non-adjustable) — bounds the rekey lifecycle.
- A service account **can exist with zero IAM bindings** (the decoupling primitive).

Sources:
- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview

## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)

memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.

A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)

A deferral-honoring breadcrumb has been added to memo-20260527's Status block now; the full
divergence note folds in when the model is frozen.

74	  pre-existing identity. Both are **intent-first**: grant writes the ledger entry then adds
75	  bindings; revoke withdraws the ledger entry then removes them. Invariant: *no IAM revoke without a
76	  prior ledger withdrawal* — a crashed teardown then lands as a transient *surplus* (reported,
77	  safe), not an auto-converging *deficit* that would resurrect the just-removed identity (acute
78	  because RBSDD anticipates immediate same-folio re-invest).
79	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
80	  - *citizen* (this heat):
81	    - **add** = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA.
82	    - **rekey** = rotate the key, bindings untouched. Availability-safe order: create-new →
83	      deliver RBRA → verify → delete-old (never delete-before-create — a failed create would leave
84	      a live citizen keyless). GCP caps an SA at **10 keys** (hard, non-adjustable), so a rekey
85	      that fails to reclaim the old key accumulates toward the ceiling; reclaim stale keys, and let
86	      the orphan/audit sweep flag `>1` USER_MANAGED key on a citizen as a stranded key to reclaim.
87	    - **remove** = delete the SA (and key). Capabilities are revoked first by divest's
88	      revoke(all held), which is intent-first (see Capability verbs), so the full teardown order is
89	      withdraw-ledger → unbind-IAM → delete-SA.
90	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
91	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
92	    delete).
93	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
94	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
95

file.content:
pre-existing identity. Both are **intent-first**: grant writes the ledger entry then adds
  bindings; revoke withdraws the ledger entry then removes them. Invariant: *no IAM revoke without a
  prior ledger withdrawal* — a crashed teardown then lands as a transient *surplus* (reported,
  safe), not an auto-converging *deficit* that would resurrect the just-removed identity (acute

- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):

aiTitle: Review federation login workflow and cloud secret handling

content.text:
Grounded now — and the mechanism memo (`memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`) already pre-decided part of this, which sharpens the answer and corrects one piece of the framing.

## The organizing move: RBRA fuses three things; split them, don't replace it

What a workstation holds for a citizen is really **three distinct things** that RBRA currently smears together:

1. **WHO am I** — the identity selector (`alice`).
2. **WHAT durable secret proves it** — tier-specific, and the tiers *never coexist* in a depot (mode enum, one per depot).
3. **The ephemeral Google access token** — ≤1h, ≤12h ceiling.

The citizen model wants (1) hoisted out and made **tier-blind**, (2) kept **tier-specific**, and (3) recognized as **not config at all**. Your RBRC/RBRG instinct is reaching for exactly this split — but the count and the labels want adjusting.

## Layer 1 — identity (your RBRC): yes, but a field, not a regime — and it's the BZ-actionable piece

You already sensed it ("could be a new RBRS element") — commit to that. It's an inert, singular, stable scalar; minting a whole regime for one name fails the load-bearing test. Make it `RBRS_CITIZEN`.

The *why* is sharper than "it's tidy," though: **in keyfile mode the identity is already derivable** (RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL is `alice@…`), so RBRC looks redundant there. It earns its existence at the **federation** tier, where there's **no key file to read the name off of** — the identity must have an explicit home. Putting it in RBRS *unconditionally* (both tiers) is what lets the identity-keyed accessor read "who am I" from one tier-blind place and fetch credential material separately.

And this is the one part of your musing that's **actionable in ₣BZ now**, because the memo already commits to it implicitly: *"RBSRA / RBSRR — the three role-keyed `rbra.env` paths collapse toward one identity-keyed path."* That collapse *needs a key* — `RBRS_CITIZEN` is that key, and RBRR's path derives from it (`…/citizens/${RBRS_CITIZEN}/rbra.env`). Three role files → one identity-keyed file, keyed on the station's citizen name.

## Layer 2 — durable secret (your RBRG): real, but three corrections

This is where I'd push back, because "RBRG for Google, replacing RBRA" mis-locates three things:

- **It doesn't replace RBRA — the memo *keeps* RBRA.** The citizen identity-verbs are literally `add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA`, `rekey = rotate → deliver RBRA`. RBRA stays as the keyfile-tier credential; what changes is multiplicity (3→1) and keying (role→identity). So RBRA is *refactored*, not retired.
- **The durable federation secret is the *IdP's* refresh token, not Google's.** Workforce federation gives Google only the *ephemeral* access token via STS; there is no durable Google credential. So a regime named "G for Google" mis-cues — the thing it holds is an IdP artifact (the `offline_access` refresh token), and it exists **only in the federation/test-rig posture** (shipped humans persist none, per R4 last turn).
- **The two never coexist.** A depot is one tier. So RBRA (keyfile) and the federation refresh token are never both present on a box — which is the argument for keeping them as **two distinct names** rather than one polymorphic file. The tier-blindness belongs in Layer 1 (identity) and the accessor, **not** in the secret-material layer, where the secrets are genuinely different (PEM signing key vs bearer refresh token; signed-JWT mint vs STS mint).

So your RBRG is real, but it's a **federation-tier sibling**, not RBRA's replacement — and I'd letter it **RBRF (Federation)** over RBRG (Google). Your "rewritten whenever a new refresh token is delivered" cadence is exactly right for it. (Note it's also distinct from the *Payor's* OAuth refresh token, which already exists in RBRO/RBRP — the memo lists `payor: install/refresh a human OAuth refresh token` as its own thing. Sibling, separate.)

## Layer 3 — the ephemeral access token: keep it out of the regime system entirely

The ≤1h Google bearer token is **runtime scratch** — a temp file or keychain entry with a TTL — not configuration. Putting it in a regime would inject hourly churn into a system meant for stable declared state. Your RBRG instinct correctly scoped itself to the *refresh* token (durable), so you already drew this line; I'm just making it explicit that the access token is on the far side of it.

**Corrected tally:** not "two new regimes replacing RBRA." It's — *refactor* RBRA (keep, 3→1, identity-keyed) **+** one new RBRS *field* (`RBRS_CITIZEN`, actionable in BZ now) **+** one *future* federation refresh-token regime (RBRF, deferred with the tier) **+** the ephemeral token explicitly *not* a regime.

## Census in the diagrams: no — wrong subsystem, and premature

Two independent reasons, both pointing the same way:

- **Wrong subsystem.** The four `rbdg*` diagrams are *authentication* — login flows and the token-accessor seam. The Census is *authorization intent*, and it is **never consulted at login or token mint**: enforcement is server-side IAM (cinched), and the Census is diffed against IAM by the **audit**, not read by the accessor. Drawing it into `rbdgl`/`rbdgk`/`rbdgm` would render a data-flow that doesn't exist and blur the "IAM is the only enforcement" line. The *one* place a Census write is honest is a capability **grant** (intent-first: "grant writes the ledger entry then adds bindings") — and that's tier-blind, so it belongs in a **grant/audit** diagram, not the federation-setup figure.
- **Premature.** Census is a *reserved* civic-ontology quoin, explicitly "NOT yet in RBS0 (premature pre-MVP)" per the paddock. Promoting it into a committed, rendered diagram now inverts the sequence — same shape as minting RBRF today. Let Census land in RBS0 first, then give it **its own diagram**: the intent-first grant/revoke flow plus the audit diff (deficit auto-converge / surplus report). That's a figure the auth family doesn't have and shouldn't absorb.

So: don't invest *these* diagrams with the Census; draw it its own when it's incorporated.

---

What's live for ₣BZ out of all this is narrow and concrete: **`RBRS_CITIZEN` as the key for the RBRA 3→1 collapse.** Everything else (RBRF, Census diagram) is federation/capability-layer work the paddock already holds at arm's length.

Want me to (1) capture this three-layer regime decomposition as a ₣BZ paddock note (it's durable design input, and we've now derived it twice-adjacent), or (2) leave it in the chat and just slate the `RBRS_CITIZEN` + RBRR-path-collapse as a concrete BZ pace when you're ready to groom toward paces?

message.content: Does the future RBRA then become basically a station singleton as opposed to its current manifold?

content.text:
Yes for the common case — but the precise statement is sharper than "singleton," and the sharpness is the interesting part.

## It's not a shrink, it's an axis rotation

Today's manifold is indexed by **role**: three RBRA files because *one person wears three role-hats* (governor / director / retriever = three SAs = three keys). That's an **intra-person** axis — it's always ≥1, and it's the *same human* fanned out across roles.

The citizen model fuses those hats: one person = one citizen = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of capabilities. So the manifold doesn't just drop 3→1 — **the reason it existed dissolves.** The role axis is gone; what's left is a manifold indexed by **citizen**, which is an **inter-person** axis. On a personal workstation there's one person → one citizen → **one RBRA**. So the collapse to a singleton is load-bearing, not incidental: it's the workstation-local shadow of the "one SA per person, not per role" cinch.

## So it's a *citizen* singleton, not a *station* singleton — and the test rig is where that bites

RBRS is a singleton *by nature* — one machine, one machine-config, full stop. RBRA's new singleton-ness is different in kind: it's **one-per-citizen**, which merely *coincides* with one-per-station because a personal box is one person.

The two notions come apart exactly on the **test rig**: a box driving multi-actor scenarios may need to *be* several distinct citizens (alice, bob, carol) at once. There RBRS stays singleton (still one machine) but RBRA goes **plural again** — re-indexed by citizen, not collapsed away. (Whether the theurge rig actually needs >1 operator-citizen on one box is the thing to confirm — it's the boundary case, and I'd verify it rather than assume.)

## What "one identity-keyed path" actually means

This reconciles the memo's wording with the test-rig caveat: *"collapse toward one identity-keyed path"* is **one path *template*, keyed by citizen** — `…/citizens/${citizen}/rbra.env` — not one literal file. Then:

- The **accessor takes identity as a parameter** ("token as alice") — that's the real seam, and it's intrinsically a manifold-addresser.
- **`RBRS_CITIZEN` is the *default*** the accessor resolves to when the caller doesn't specify — the personal-box common case, where it instantiates the template exactly once → the singleton you're describing.
- The **test harness overrides per-actor**, instantiating the template N times.

So "RBRA becomes a station singleton" is true read as *"the default resolves to one on a personal box,"* and false read as *"RBRA is intrinsically one-per-machine like RBRS."* The manifold is preserved underneath — it just rotated from role-indexed (always ≥1, every box) to citizen-indexed (1 on a personal box, N only where a box impersonates several people).

One-liner: **RBRA stops being a per-role manifold every workstation carries, and becomes a per-citizen manifold that is singleton-by-default — collapsed to one wherever one person operates the box, still plural wherever the rig wears many faces.**

This is the same `RBRS_CITIZEN`-as-default-key shape from last turn, now with the caveat that it's a *default over a manifold*, not a hard singleton — worth getting into the paddock note precisely that way if we capture it, so the test-rig plurality isn't accidentally designed out.

message.content: A rule I insist upon is that either a regime (RBRx.env) is edited by human _or_ it is maintained by the infrastructure with no direct human edit.  Are you saying RBRA becomes full auto in citizen model?

A rule I insist upon is that either a regime (RBRx.env) is edited by human _or_ it is maintained by the infrastructure with no direct human edit.  Are you saying RBRA becomes full auto in citizen model?  To be clear, if several users share a workstation, they _must_ have distinct home directories or filesystems- I am not supporting other.

content.text:
Yes — and your rule is exactly the right blade to hold against it, because it catches the one way the citizen model could go wrong.

## RBRA goes full-auto — that's the direct consequence of decoupling

In the citizen model RBRA's sole authors are the identity verbs: `add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA`, `rekey = rotate → deliver RBRA → verify → delete-old`. The operator never opens it. So yes — **RBRA flips to infrastructure-maintained, no direct human edit.** That's not a side effect; it's the whole point of pulling identity lifecycle into verbs — the credential becomes a *managed artifact*, not a hand-tended file. (Whether it's *cleanly* human-authored today or already a bit mixed is worth pinning down, but the target state is unambiguous: full auto.)

## Your rule has teeth here — it forbids the mixed state, which forces an eviction

This is where the rule does real work. RBRA is only clean if it's *purely* infra-owned — and today there's a suspect human knob riding in it: **`RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC`** (defaulted, validated to 300–3600 — i.e. overridable). If that stays, citizen-model RBRA becomes the forbidden hybrid: infra writes the key, a human tunes the lifetime. Your XOR says: **evict it.** The lifetime knob moves to a human-edited regime, and RBRA is left as nothing but verb-delivered credential material.

So the three-layer split from earlier isn't just tidy — **your rule *forces* it:**

- **`RBRS_CITIZEN` (+ any lifetime knob) → human-edited.** The operator declares "this home acts as alice" and sets tunables. RBRS stays human-side, uncontaminated.
- **RBRA → full-auto.** Infra delivers the key to the path *derived from* the human-declared `RBRS_CITIZEN`.

That's a clean handshake that honors the XOR per file: human edits the *selector*, infra owns the *credential*, and they're different files. The selector being separate from RBRA isn't a stylistic choice anymore — it's the only way RBRA can be full-auto without dragging a human field along.

## Your shared-workstation rule simplifies what I overbuilt last turn

I'll concede this cleanly: my "RBRA stays a manifold-by-citizen within one box" hedge is **moot under your constraint.** If distinct users *must* have distinct homes/filesystems, then a regime's scope (a home) is single-citizen by fiat — so RBRA is a **true singleton per home**, full stop. The multi-citizen plurality I described exists only *across* homes, which are separate regime universes that never share an RBRA path. The "default over a manifold" framing was solving a problem you've ruled out of existence. Good — it means `RBRS_CITIZEN` is a genuine singleton selector, not a default, and the accessor reads it rather than needing an identity parameter for the operator case. (The test rig, if it drives several citizens, does so as several homes — same rule, no special-casing.)

So the net is simpler and rule-clean: **one home = one citizen = one full-auto RBRA, its lifecycle owned entirely by `add`/`rekey`; every human-set value lives next door in human-edited RBRS.**

Want me to confirm whether `RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC` (or anything else in RBRA today) is actually human-tunable — that's the concrete audit your rule demands before RBRA can be declared clean-auto?

message.content: I'm musing a configuration model where Governor picks the lifetime of the access/ retrieval token following 'human citizen login', and that a special Payor operation can greatly extend that for use in CI/CD scenarios.  thoughts?

content.text:
Two halves, and they have different mechanics — the Governor half is a genuinely good new knob (it even *homes* the field we said had to leave RBRA), but the Payor half needs one correction that changes its security shape entirely.

## The correction first: "greatly extend" can't mean a longer token

There are hard ceilings neither Governor nor Payor can exceed — they're Palisade limits, Google-engineered:

- **Keyfile:** SA-signed-JWT access token caps at **1h**.
- **Federation:** Workforce session caps at **12h**.

So "Payor greatly extends the lifetime for CI/CD" cannot mean *a single token that lives for a month* — that token doesn't exist. Past the ceiling, extension is only achievable by **re-minting**: a persisted refresh token the box silently re-exchanges every ≤12h. Which means your Payor operation isn't "set TTL = 30 days" — it's "authorize emplacing a refresh token on a firewalled CI box." That's **R4 from two turns ago**, restated as a config operation, and you've landed on the same structure independently — a good sign. But the security properties only hold if it's a refresh-token posture, not a fat token: each token stays short and **centrally revocable** (disable the IdP principal → every CI box dies at once); the durable secret is the refresh token, firewall-caged. A long-lived *access* token would be an un-revocable fat bearer secret — the thing the whole tier exists to avoid.

## "Following human citizen login" is federation-shaped by construction

Keyfile has **no login event** — the SA key is just present, every tabtarget mints fresh. There's no session to cap. So your model is really describing the **federation** session-lifetime policy. In keyfile the analog degenerates to the per-mint `exp` (≤1h), which the station sets at JWT-mint time.

## The convergence — this *closes* last turn's open question

Here's why your musing is more than a tangent: two turns ago we said `RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC` **must be evicted** from a full-auto RBRA, and left "where does it go?" open. Your model answers it: **the lifetime becomes Governor-administered policy, not a human station field.** Concretely —

- **Keyfile:** the lifetime policy lives in **RBRD** (depot regime, tripwire-protected), and the station's mint reads it to set `exp`. Tamper-evident, so a compromised station can't extend its own window.
- **Federation:** it's the Workforce session duration the Governor/Payor configures at the pool.

Same intent, two homes — and it satisfies your XOR rule *better* than a human-edited `RBRS_LIFETIME` would, because it's centrally administered, not station-local. So the eviction target isn't "human edits a station knob" — it's "Governor policy." Nice closure.

## Guardrails the model should carry

- **Tamper-evident baseline** (rides RBRD's tripwire) — the station honors the lifetime, can't raise it.
- **Payor escalation is a logged, auditable standing exception** — per the Palisade "log the bend" discipline and the audit's surplus ethos, it should be *visible* that a given box wears an extended-CI posture, not silently long-lived.
- **Escalation produces re-exchange, not a fat token** (the correction above).

## One honest caveat on where the knob earns its keep

In **federation** the access-token window *is* the only exposure, so a Governor lifetime knob is genuinely load-bearing. In **keyfile** the **SA key on disk dwarfs the token** as the real durable risk — shaving the access-token TTL from 1h to 15min buys little when the key itself re-mints forever. So: implement it in keyfile (via RBRD) for uniformity, but don't over-invest there; its security value is a federation-tier property.

*(Optional, possibly premature: your "access/**retrieval** token" phrasing hints at lifetime keyed by **capability-set** — a low-blast-radius retriever token allowed a longer window than a high-blast-radius governor token. That'd tie directly to the blast-radius cinch. Interesting, but I'd flag it as a refinement to hold, not build now.)*

---

This is still federation-era for the Governor/Payor policy itself, but the **BZ-actionable shadow is concrete: the evicted `RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC` resolves to a Governor-administered RBRD field, not a station knob.** Want me to capture *that* specific resolution (lifetime → tamper-evident RBRD policy; Payor escalation = refresh-token enrollment, federation-deferred) as the answer to the open eviction question — either as a ₣BZ paddock note or just banked here for when you slate paces?

message.content: I'm still mulling how _inside_ the firewall, we can tweak the system to avoid the human involvement for CI/CD in federation.  Is there a way we can locally break the 'human must click the browser button' rule without changing the sequence of tabtargets involved?  Hence a special tabtarget perhaps, or a 'hidden' command

content.text:
Yes — but the clean version inverts your framing slightly, and that inversion is the whole trick: **you don't break the click rule on every run; you satisfy it *once*, out of band, and capture its durable byproduct so the operating sequence never needs it again.** Let me lay out why that's both achievable and the *only* clean form, then answer special-tabtarget-vs-hidden-command directly.

## The click isn't bypassed — it's amortized, and its residue is the refresh token

The human click does two things: authenticates the human to the IdP, and consents. Its byproduct, *if you ask for `offline_access`*, is a **refresh token**. That refresh token is exactly the artifact that lets every later run go headless: redeem refresh token → fresh ID token (Leg 1 with **no human**) → STS exchange → access token. So "skip the click" has a precise, sanctioned meaning — **hold a refresh token** — and any other meaning is worse (more on that below).

## Why the tabtarget sequence stays byte-identical: the credential source is *ambient*, not a flag

This is the elegant part, and it's already in the design (memo-20260527, "One consumer path, real and synthetic"). The token accessor resolves "give me a token as alice" by consulting a **credential source** configured at *station scope*. That source is pluggable:

- **Real human:** interactive device-flow session → triggers the click.
- **Synthetic human (CI):** an **executable-sourced credential** — the same pattern Google documents for Workforce ("file- and executable-sourced credentials"), realized in your curl-native accessor as a pluggable "obtain ID token" step that redeems the caged refresh token.

The operating tabtargets call the accessor; the accessor reads the *ambient* station config; in CI that config points at the refresh-token-redeeming source. **No flag, no hidden command, no modified invocation in the operating sequence** — the difference is entirely pre-emplaced ambient config. That's the strongest form of your "without changing the sequence": the tabtargets aren't even passed a CI marker. This *is* "they differ only in the credential source."

## So: special tabtarget — yes. Hidden inline command — no.

Your "special tabtarget perhaps, or a hidden command" splits cleanly, and your instinct is right under one reading:

- **The special/​"hidden" tabtarget = the one-time *enrollment* verb.** Run once on the firewalled box, *with a human present that one time*, requesting `offline_access`; it captures the refresh token and writes the credential-source config. It's "hidden" in the correct sense — **not in the shipped register** (memo-20260527: "a separate test-only enrollment verb, never in the shipped register"; R4's "log a machine in for a month"). It's out of band from the operating sequence, not inside it.
- **Reject the "hidden inline auto-click every run."** To auto-approve the click on every run you'd need either a headless browser robot driving the IdP consent UI (Palisade-fragile — breaks on every IdP redesign, scrapes a foreign UI you can't govern) or an embedded scriptable IdP credential (a *worse* durable secret than a clean refresh token). Both reconstruct the on-disk-secret you were escaping, messily. The refresh token **is** the clean form of "skip the click"; there's no magic that avoids *some* durable artifact — only the choice of a clean, revocable, scoped one vs. a hacky one.

## Three honest residues — none fatal, all worth designing for

1. **The click recurs at the refresh-token's TTL.** IdP refresh tokens expire (days/weeks/sliding). When it does, the headless redeem fails and the box needs **re-enrollment** — one human click, ~monthly. Amortized, not eliminated (R4's "human touch happens once, when the token is minted"). The operating sequence still never contains it.

2. **Fail-fast is mandatory, and it's a real design constraint.** The CI credential source must **hard-fail** on refresh-token expiry/revocation — it must *never* degrade to an interactive device-flow prompt, because a prompt in a headless box hangs forever waiting for a click that won't come. So the source is mode-aware: human → prompt on expiry; CI → crash-fast with "re-enroll." That's a Palisade membrane — absorb only the valid-refresh-token happy path, die loud on everything else.

3. **The irreducible one (sincerity):** the device-flow *human-interaction leg itself* cannot be automated — a click is by definition not headless. So CI validates everything *downstream* of token acquisition (STS exchange, the consumer path, GCP calls), but the interaction leg is exercised only by the real human at enrollment. "Synthetic human walks the same path" holds for the consumer path; it does **not** hold for the click leg. That leg's only test is the once-a-month real click. Worth knowing you're not getting automated coverage of it, ever — that's structural, not a gap to close.

---

Federation-era, so out of ₣BZ scope — but the BZ-relevant shadow is the same one from earlier, now with sharper teeth: **the accessor must treat the credential source as opaque and pluggable from day one** (not "every call mints fresh from a key"), or the executable source can't slot in later without reshaping the seam. That's the one thing BZ's keyfile-only accessor must get right to keep this CI path cheap when federation lands.

Want me to bank this — the "enrollment-once + ambient executable credential source + fail-fast-never-prompt" shape — as the concrete answer to "how does federation CI stay headless," either folded into the ₣BZ paddock as a federation-seam constraint or held in the memo trail for when federation is scoped?

aiTitle: Review federation login workflow and cloud secret handling

message.content: I like the confidence with which you speak, while simultaneously I am not following the technical dialog.  However, I am electing to trust you with more and more detail.  I think you are saying that each long test run dispatched locally or remotely is accompanied by a human step, and I can live with that, because its a human step to kick off a test run, and maybe that's even accomodatable within a claude code session without obligating me to paste a secret into claude code because the browser auth step has no secrets.  Do I have it right?  If so lets figure out how to commit to this plan.  I think there are too many memos and paddocks- I do not have them straight in my head.  I'd like your thought, but I'm musing we ought to pick a primary memo to hold the final federation shape in all bloody detail.  ?

content.text:
Let me repay the trust the right way — by being plain rather than smooth, including where it means correcting your restatement. And the correction is good news: the place you've landed is *simpler* than what I'd been describing.

## You have it right — and the version you described needs no stored secret at all

Here's the thing I should make clear, because I'd been answering a harder case than you actually have. There are two situations, and they're very different:

- **A human is there when the run needs to authenticate** (someone kicks off the run, and is around again if it runs long). The human does one browser click at the start; that click yields a session that covers the run; **nothing durable is stored anywhere.** No refresh token, no caged secret, none of the machinery I spent three turns on. This is exactly what you described — "a human step to kick off a test run" — and your instinct that you "can live with it" is right, because it's the *cleanest* option, not a compromise.
- **Nobody is there** (a 3am scheduled run, no human to click). *Then* you need the stored refresh token — the one durable secret, firewall-caged, re-enrolled ~monthly. This is the only case that needs the complicated part.

So the refresh-token complexity isn't intrinsic to federation CI — it's the price of **nobody being present**. If your runs are human-kicked, you can drop it from the plan entirely. I'd been describing the unattended case because the memos assume it; your actual constraint is the easier one.

## Your Claude Code instinct is correct, and here's exactly why it's secret-free

The device flow hands the orchestrator only a **URL and a short code** — neither is a secret (they're single-use and useless without the human authenticating to their *own* IdP). So a Claude Code session can run the tabtarget, show you "click here," you authenticate in your own browser, and the run proceeds. **You never paste a secret into Claude Code, because the browser step has none to paste.** The access token that results lives inside the tabtarget process Claude Code spawned — not in the chat. So your model is sound: Claude Code kicks off an authenticated run on one secret-free human click. That's a real, clean pattern, not a workaround.

## The one fork that decides the plan

Everything hinges on a single question only you can answer, about your actual workflow:

> **When a long test run needs to authenticate, is a human there?**

> - **Sometimes no** (scheduled/unattended runs with nobody home) → you need the caged refresh token *for those runs only*, with all the firewall/fail-fast machinery.

My read of what you've said: you're describing the **first**. If that's right, the plan gets dramatically smaller.

## On the memo sprawl — my thought, and a reframe that should help

You don't have them straight because they genuinely aren't. But I think it collapses to **two live things** in your head, and the rest is history:

1. **What you're building now** = the citizen/keyfile tier = the **₣BZ paddock** + `memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model`. One heat, one mechanism memo.
2. **The future federation shape** = `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models`. One memo — *already* designated "the source document" for the roadmap.

Everything else (`memo-20260522` with its R4/R5, the leak memos) is **derivation** — the reasoning that produced decisions. It should be *pointed to*, not *held in your head*.

So my recommendation matches your musing exactly: **elevate `memo-20260527` to be the single federation canon — the final shape in all bloody detail.** It already has the mandate; it's just stale (marked "not yet revised") and missing this conversation's refinements. Crucially:

## What I'd propose, in order

1. **You answer the fork above** (human-there / sometimes-not / both). That's the keystone — the canon can't be written without it.
2. **Then I draft the consolidation**: revise `memo-20260527` into the federation canon — fold in the fork's outcome, the regime decomposition, the lifetime policy, and the headless-CI mechanism (only as far as your fork needs it) — with a short front-matter map saying what supersedes what and what to point-to-not-read.
3. **You review before anything commits.**

I won't touch a file until you've called the fork and said go. So: **when a long run needs to authenticate — is a human there?**
